title: Floquet engineering of tight-binding Hamiltonians in momentum space lattices
authors: D. Ronco, F. Arrouas, N. Ombredane, E. Flament, Q. Levoy, B. Peaudecerf, D. Guéry-Odelin
abstract: Quantum simulation with ultracold atoms provides a versatile platform to emulate condensed-matter models. In particular, momentum-space lattices enable the realization of programmable tight-binding Hamiltonians. Here, we generalize this approach by exploiting quantum resonances of a periodically driven (shaken) rotor within the Floquet framework. Using first-order time-dependent perturbation theory, we derive analytical relations between the lattice modulation and the effective tight-binding parameters, and identify explicit solutions for several resonances. We further apply optimal-control techniques to enhance the multi-period Floquet fidelity and extend the accessible parameter regimes. Experimentally, we implement this scheme with a Bose-Einstein condensate of rubidium-87 atoms in a dynamically modulated optical lattice. We demonstrate the simulation of the Rice-Mele model, including band-structure measurements and topological edge states, as well as momentum Bloch oscillations, and superlattice configurations with controlled periodicity. Our results establish quantum resonances as a powerful resource for Floquet engineering of tight-binding models in momentum space.
summary: This paper presents a method to engineer programmable tight-binding Hamiltonians in momentum space using a shaken rotor of ultracold atoms. By exploiting quantum resonances and optimal control, the researchers can independently tune tunneling amplitudes and on-site energies. They experimentally demonstrate the simulation of the Rice-Mele model, topological edge states, and Bloch oscillations. This approach turns quantum resonances into a versatile resource for quantum simulation.
main_problem: Developing a programmable method for Floquet engineering of tight-binding Hamiltonians in momentum-space lattices by independently tuning on-site energies and tunneling amplitudes.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that quantum resonances in a shaken rotor can be used to engineer various models, including the Rice-Mele model, and show that optimal control can significantly enhance multi-period Floquet fidelity.
method: The study uses Floquet theory, first-order time-dependent perturbation theory, and a kernel-based inversion method, supplemented by gradient-ascent optimal control techniques.
model_or_system: A Bose-Einstein condensate of Rubidium-87 atoms in a dynamically modulated (shaken) optical lattice, modeled by a generalized 'shaken rotor' Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Momentum distribution, site populations, momentum width, average momentum, and Floquet operator fidelity.
important_parameters: Effective Planck constant (hbar_eff), kick period (T), lattice depth (s0), and modulation functions (f1, f2).
main_assumptions: The framework assumes a perturbative regime (weak driving) and relies on quantum resonance conditions to maintain discrete translational symmetry.
figures_summary: Figures show the real and imaginary parts of modulation functions, comparison between perturbative and optimal control solutions, and experimental observations of momentum-space Bloch oscillations, topological edge states, and wave packet expansion in superlattices.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from theoretical derivation of the Floquet framework and analytical relations to the implementation of optimal control, followed by experimental verification of various tight-binding models and topological phenomena.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful toolkit for simulating complex condensed-matter models like the Rice-Mele model and topological junctions using the highly controllable platform of momentum-space lattices in ultracold atoms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Real (solid line) and imaginary (dotted line) parts of the modulation function f(t) used to simulate a seven-site Rice–Mele model with a uniform tunneling am- plitude t0 = 0.7 ℏeff, at a ℏeff = 2π resonance. (b) Real part of the effective Hamiltonian, Re[Hn,m/ℏeff], associated with the modulation in panel (a). (c) Effective Hamilto- nian parameters: on-site energies Hn,n/ℏeff for even (down blue triangles) and odd (up blue triangles) sites n, and first- (Hn,n+1/ℏeff, green circles) and second- (Hn,n+2/ℏeff, orange squares) nearest-neighbor tunneling amplitudes. (d) Fidelity between the Floquet operator generated from Eq. (20) and the target Rice–Mele time-evolution operator,...

Figure 2. (a) Comparison between the modulation function obtained from first-order perturbation theory (dotted line) and its optimal-control refinement (solid line) at the quantum resonance ℏeff = 6π, for a 9-site Rice–Mele model with uniform tunneling amplitude t0 = 0.7 ℏeff. (b) Floquet-operator fidelity as a function of the number of repeated periods, for different tunneling amplitudes and Nh = 9, comparing the perturbative solution (dotted line) and the optimal-control solution (solid line). (c) Time-averaged fidelity over Nt = 5 periods as a function of the tunneling amplitude, for chain sizes Nh = 7 and 11, comparing the perturbative (dotted line) and optimal-control (solid line)...

Figure 3. (a) Probability evolution map of a wave packet ini- tially localized on site |0⟩in the exact 9-site Rice–Mele model with ℏeff = 6π and tunneling amplitude t0 = 0.7 ℏeff. (b) Probability evolution map of the same initial state under the driven dynamics of Eq. (8), using the optimal-control mod- ulation shown in Fig. (2)a). (c) Time evolution of selected site populations in the exact Rice–Mele model (solid lines) compared with the optimal-control simulation (dotted lines). Optimal control parameters : MT = 300, N = 17 and Nt = 5.

Figure 4. Experimental measurement of a quantum walk in a Rice-Mele model with ℏeff = 2π so ∆= π × ℏeff, t0 = 0.7 × ℏeff = t1, 7 sites and an initial state |1⟩with a modulation function improved by the optimal control method. Comparison between the time evolution of the theoretical probability (full lines) and experimental data (markers) of the probability of being on site |0⟩(green and square dots), |1⟩ (orange and round dots) and |3⟩(blue and triangular dots). The Floquet period is T2π ≈61.6 µs and the optimal con- trol parameters used to obtain the modulation function are MT = 1000, N = 21 and Nt = 5.

Figure 5. (a) Prepared fundamental state distribution: ex- perimental absorption image of the momentum orders after a 35 ms time of flight. (b,c) Experimental observation of the coherent oscillations of the initial ground state in a seven-site Rice–Mele model at ℏeff = 2π (∆= π ℏeff) with tunneling amplitude t0 = 0.6 ℏeff = t1, using an optimal-control modu- lation. Single-shot measurements of the site populations are shown for |0⟩(green squares), |1⟩(red triangles), | −1⟩(or- ange triangles), |2⟩(purple circles), and | −2⟩(blue circles). The Floquet period is T2π ≈61.6 µs and the optimal con- trol parameters used to obtain the modulation function are MT = 1000, N = 21 and Nt = 5.

(a) Time evolution of the momentum distribution for an initial state localized on site |0⟩in a tight-binding model with tunneling amplitude t0/ℏeff = 0.8 and effective uniform force ωB = 1.4. (b) Time evolution of the populations of sites |0⟩(blue circles), |1⟩(orange triangles), and |2⟩(green squares), averaged over five experimental runs. The analytical prediction from Eq. Figure 9. (a,b) Time evolution of the momentum distribu- tions from initial states |1⟩and | −2⟩, respectively, in a tight- binding model with three-site periodicity (ℏeff = 16π/3) and nearest-neighbor tunneling amplitude t0/ℏeff = 0.9. (c) Time evolution of selected site populations: for the initial state |1⟩, sites |1⟩(blue circles) and |2⟩(orange triangles); for the initial state | −2⟩, sites | −2⟩(green squares) and | −1⟩. Data are averaged over five experimental runs. The Floquet period is T16π/3 ≈164.4 µs and the optimal control parameters used to obtain the modulation function are MT = 1000, N = 21 and Nt = 5.
